_note: AIVORA-authored original practice MCQs for UPSC Prelims. Questions written from standard, verifiable general-studies facts in our own words; not copied from any coaching material.

questions.text: Which Article of the Indian Constitution is described by Dr. B. R. Ambedkar as the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 32 guarantees the right to constitutional remedies, allowing citizens to move the Supreme Court directly for enforcement of Fundamental Rights. Ambedkar called it the heart and soul of the Constitution because rights are meaningless without a remedy to enforce them.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements regarding the Directive Principles of State Policy:
1. They are enforceable by the courts.
2. They are inspired by the Constitution of Ireland.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Directive Principles (Part IV) are non-justiciable, meaning they cannot be enforced by the courts, so statement 1 is wrong. They were borrowed from the Irish Constitution, so statement 2 is correct.

questions.text: The words 'Socialist' and 'Secular' were added to the Preamble of the Constitution by which amendment?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment Act of 1976 added the words 'Socialist', 'Secular', and 'Integrity' to the Preamble.

questions.text: A Money Bill under the Indian Constitution can be introduced only in the:
questions.explanation: As per Article 110, a Money Bill can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha. The Rajya Sabha can make recommendations, which the Lok Sabha may accept or reject, and must return the bill within 14 days.

questions.text: The Right to Property was removed from the list of Fundamental Rights and made a legal right by which amendment?
questions.explanation: The 44th Amendment Act of 1978 removed the Right to Property from Part III and made it a legal right under Article 300A.

questions.text: The doctrine of 'basic structure' of the Constitution was propounded by the Supreme Court in which case?
questions.explanation: The basic structure doctrine was established in the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), holding that Parliament cannot amend the essential features of the Constitution.

questions.text: Which Schedule of the Constitution deals with the division of powers between the Union and the States through three lists?
questions.explanation: The Seventh Schedule contains the Union List, State List, and Concurrent List, dividing legislative powers. The Eighth Schedule lists recognised languages.

questions.text: The 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendment Acts are associated with:
questions.explanation: The 73rd Amendment gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj Institutions (rural) and the 74th to Municipalities (urban), both in 1992.

questions.text: The President of India is elected by an electoral college consisting of:
questions.option_a: Elected members of both Houses of Parliament only
questions.option_b: Elected members of Parliament and elected members of State Legislative Assemblies
questions.option_c: All members of Parliament and State legislatures
questions.option_d: Members of the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Councils
questions.explanation: The electoral college comprises the elected members of both Houses of Parliament and the elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of States (and certain Union Territories). Nominated members do not participate.

questions.text: Which of the following is a constitutional body?
questions.explanation: The Finance Commission is a constitutional body established under Article 280. NITI Aayog is an executive body; the NHRC and CIC are statutory bodies.

questions.text: The Revolt of 1857 began at which of the following places?
questions.explanation: The revolt broke out at Meerut on 10 May 1857, after which the rebels marched to Delhi and proclaimed Bahadur Shah Zafar as their leader.

questions.text: The practice of Sati was legally abolished in 1829 during the governor-generalship of:
questions.explanation: Sati was abolished by Regulation XVII of 1829 under Lord William Bentinck, following the reform efforts of Raja Ram Mohan Roy.

questions.text: The 'Drain of Wealth' theory was expounded by:
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji developed the drain of wealth theory in his work 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India', arguing that Britain was economically draining India.

questions.text: The Non-Cooperation Movement was withdrawn by Mahatma Gandhi following which incident?
questions.explanation: Gandhi called off the Non-Cooperation Movement in February 1922 after a mob set fire to a police station at Chauri Chaura, killing policemen, as he was committed to non-violence.

questions.text: The partition of Bengal, which triggered the Swadeshi Movement, was carried out in 1905 by:
questions.explanation: Lord Curzon announced the partition of Bengal in 1905, which sparked widespread protest and the Swadeshi and Boycott movement.

questions.text: The Dandi March of 1930 was associated with the breaking of which law?
questions.explanation: The Dandi March marked the start of the Civil Disobedience Movement, in which Gandhi broke the salt law by making salt from seawater at Dandi.

questions.text: Which session of the Indian National Congress witnessed the split between the Moderates and the Extremists?
questions.explanation: The Congress split into Moderates and Extremists at the Surat session of 1907. The two factions reunited at the Lucknow session of 1916.

questions.text: The slogan 'Do or Die' is associated with which movement?
questions.explanation: Gandhi gave the call 'Do or Die' at the launch of the Quit India Movement on 8 August 1942.

questions.text: The Simon Commission was boycotted by Indians mainly because:
questions.option_d: It proposed dyarchy at the centre
questions.explanation: The Simon Commission (1928) was boycotted with the slogan 'Simon Go Back' because it was an all-British commission with no Indian representation.

questions.text: The Indian National Army (Azad Hind Fauj) is most closely associated with:
questions.explanation: Subhas Chandra Bose reorganised and led the Indian National Army during the Second World War to fight for India's independence from outside.

questions.text: The southwest monsoon that brings most of India's annual rainfall occurs during which months?
questions.explanation: The southwest monsoon sets in around June and withdraws by September, delivering the bulk of India's rainfall due to moisture-laden winds from the Indian Ocean.

questions.text: The Himalayas are an example of which type of mountains?
questions.explanation: The Himalayas are young fold mountains formed by the collision of the Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates.

questions.text: Which of the following peninsular rivers flows westward into the Arabian Sea?
questions.explanation: Most peninsular rivers flow eastward into the Bay of Bengal, but the Narmada and Tapi are exceptions that flow westward into the Arabian Sea through rift valleys.

questions.text: The ozone layer that protects the Earth from harmful ultraviolet radiation is found in which layer of the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: The ozone layer lies in the stratosphere and absorbs most of the Sun's ultraviolet radiation.

questions.text: Black soil (regur), well suited for cotton cultivation, is chiefly found in which region of India?
questions.explanation: Black or regur soil is derived from volcanic (Deccan trap) rocks and is ideal for cotton, found largely in Maharashtra, Gujarat, and Madhya Pradesh.

questions.text: Which of the following is a biodiversity hotspot located in India?
questions.explanation: The Western Ghats is one of the world's recognised biodiversity hotspots, along with the Eastern Himalayas, Indo-Burma, and Sundaland regions relevant to India.

questions.text: Weather phenomena such as clouds, rainfall, and storms occur mainly in which atmospheric layer?
questions.explanation: The troposphere is the lowest layer of the atmosphere and contains almost all weather phenomena.

questions.text: The phenomenon of El Niño, which can weaken the Indian monsoon, is associated with the warming of which ocean?
questions.explanation: El Niño refers to the abnormal warming of the central and eastern Pacific Ocean, which is often linked to weaker Indian monsoon rainfall.

questions.text: Kharif crops in India are sown in which season?
questions.explanation: Kharif crops such as rice and cotton are sown with the onset of the southwest monsoon (around June) and harvested in autumn.

questions.text: The Western Ghats and Eastern Ghats meet at which hills?
questions.explanation: The Western and Eastern Ghats converge at the Nilgiri Hills in southern India.

questions.text: In India, the monetary policy is formulated and implemented by:
questions.option_d: The Securities and Exchange Board of India
questions.explanation: The Reserve Bank of India is the monetary authority and formulates monetary policy, using tools such as the repo rate and CRR to control inflation and money supply.

questions.text: The fiscal deficit of a government is best defined as:
questions.option_a: The excess of revenue expenditure over revenue receipts
questions.option_b: The excess of total expenditure over total receipts excluding borrowings
questions.option_c: The fiscal deficit minus interest payments
questions.option_d: The excess of capital receipts over capital expenditure
questions.explanation: Fiscal deficit is the difference between the government's total expenditure and its total receipts excluding borrowings; it indicates the total borrowing requirement. Option A defines revenue deficit and option C defines primary deficit.

questions.text: The Goods and Services Tax (GST) in India is an example of which type of tax?
questions.explanation: GST is an indirect tax levied on the supply of goods and services, administered through the GST Council, and it replaced many earlier indirect taxes.

questions.text: The economic reforms of 1991 in India are commonly referred to as:
questions.explanation: The 1991 reforms are known as LPG — Liberalisation, Privatisation, and Globalisation — which opened up the Indian economy.

questions.text: The Planning Commission of India was replaced in 2015 by:
questions.explanation: NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) replaced the Planning Commission in 2015 as a policy think-tank.

questions.text: According to the 'ten percent law' of energy flow in an ecosystem, energy transferred from one trophic level to the next is approximately:
questions.explanation: Only about 10 percent of the energy at one trophic level is passed on to the next; the rest is lost mainly as heat during metabolism.

questions.text: The Ramsar Convention is concerned with the conservation of:
questions.explanation: The Ramsar Convention is an international treaty for the conservation and sustainable use of wetlands of international importance.

questions.text: Which of the following gases is NOT considered a major greenhouse gas?
questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide, methane, and nitrous oxide are major greenhouse gases. Oxygen is not a greenhouse gas.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements about inflation measurement in India:
1. The Consumer Price Index measures retail-level price changes.
2. The Wholesale Price Index measures price changes at the wholesale level.
Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. The CPI captures retail price changes faced by consumers, while the WPI captures price changes at the wholesale level.

questions.text: Project Tiger, launched to conserve the tiger population in India, was initiated in:
questions.explanation: Project Tiger was launched in 1973 to protect tigers and their habitats through a network of tiger reserves.
